title: The Set Secrets Behind Kung Fu Hustle And Shaolin Soccer That Made Them Legendary
dek: Wire rigs, retired kung fu legends, and a studio that tried to butcher one of them beyond recognition — here's what really happened behind the camera.

items.heading: Stephen Chow Basically Staged A Comeback Tour For Retired 1970s Kung Fu Stars
items.text: Kung Fu Hustle is packed with actors who'd been out of the spotlight for decades before Chow cast them. Bruce Leung Siu-lung, a martial arts star who'd retired in the 1980s, came back as 'The Beast' and found renewed popularity among younger audiences because of it, while Yuen Qiu's ear-splitting landlady became an instant icon in her own right.

images.caption: Bruce Leung Siu-lung, pulled out of retirement to play The Beast

items.heading: They Built An Entire Fake Slum Just To Blow It Up With Kung Fu
items.text: Production on Kung Fu Hustle ran from June through November 2003, largely at a Shanghai film studio where crews built the elaborate Pigsty Alley set from scratch. Fight choreography came courtesy of Yuen Woo-ping and Sammo Hung, and some sequences — like a casino getting demolished by a sonic 'Lion's Roar' kung fu blast — were rendered entirely in CGI because no practical stunt could pull it off.

items.heading: Shaolin Soccer Cost Almost Nothing And Still Became Hong Kong's Biggest Movie Ever
items.text: Made on a comparatively modest $10 million budget, Shaolin Soccer stomped every other release in Hong Kong in 2001, pulling in over HK$60 million domestically to become the territory's highest-grossing local film at the time. It swept that year's Hong Kong Film Awards too, taking home Best Film and Best Director for Chow.

items.heading: Miramax Bought Shaolin Soccer, Then Sat On It For Two Years Trying To 'Fix' It
items.text: Harvey Weinstein's Miramax snapped up the U.S. rights before the film had even opened in Hong Kong, then delayed release repeatedly while trimming the 112-minute cut down to roughly 87 minutes and dubbing it into English. The studio even floated renaming it 'Kung-Fu Soccer' for American audiences, a plan fans revolted against until the original title was restored.

images.caption: Stephen Chow, who also dubbed his own English voice track

items.heading: Kung Fu Hustle Made More Money On Opening Weekend Than Shaolin Soccer Made In Its Entire Run
items.text: When Kung Fu Hustle hit Asian theaters, it shattered records almost immediately — setting the biggest opening-day box office ever for a Chinese-language film in Singapore and breaking opening-day records in both Hong Kong and Taiwan. Its opening weekend alone outgrossed Shaolin Soccer's entire theatrical run, and a proper release through Sony Pictures Classics finally gave Chow the international breakthrough that Miramax had fumbled the first time around.
